segments.purgatorio-27-g56: This short clause addresses a person by name (Volencio) followed by the infinitive 'star' and the locative 'di qua'. The sequence is Name + infinitive + prepositional locative, functioning as a brusque question or command: 'Volencio, (will you) stay here?'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g57: A simple punctuation mark (semicolon) marking a pause between the previous speech and the following narrative action; it separates clauses but has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-27-g58: 'Indi' is an adverb meaning 'then' (here treated as narrative sequence). 'Sorrise' is the passato remoto, third person singular of 'sorridere' ('he smiled'). The clause 'come al fanciul si fa' is an impersonal simile: 'come ... si fa' = 'as one does to/with ...'; 'al fanciul' is the archaic form of 'al fanciullo' ('to the child'). 'Ch’ è vinto al pome' is a relative clause ('who is overcome by the apple'): 'ch’' = 'che' (who/which), 'è' = present indicative 'is', 'vinto' = past participle 'overcome', and 'al pome' gives the instrument/agental notion ('by/with the apple').
segments.purgatorio-27-g59: 'Poi' is a temporal adverb 'then'. 'Dentro al foco' is a locative prepositional phrase 'inside the fire' (with the contraction 'al' = a + il). 'Innanzi' means 'in front' or 'before'; the clitics 'mi si' with the verb 'mise' (passato remoto of 'mettere') form a reflexive/mediopassive pattern: 'si mise' = 'he placed himself', and 'mi' marks the reference 'before me'—literally 'he set himself before me'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g60: 'Pregando' is a present participle/gerund of 'pregare' here meaning 'begging' or 'entreating'. It introduces the content of his action: he was entreating Statius. 'Stazio' is the addressee (a proper name); 'che venisse' is a subordinate clause with 'che' + imperfect subjunctive 'venisse' (used after expressions of request/entreaty) meaning 'that he come' or 'to come'. 'Retro' is an adverb 'back/behind' indicating the direction 'come after/behind'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g61: This relative clause begins with 'che' (here 'who/which') and gives background: 'pria' is an adverb 'previously/earlier' (archaic spelling); 'per lunga strada' = 'along/through a long road' (per + adjective + noun), 'ci' = 'us' (object clitic), and 'divise' is the passato remoto, third person singular of 'dividere' meaning 'divided' or 'parted'. Together: 'who earlier had parted us along a long road.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g62: Sì com' fui dentro is an idiomatic temporal clause: Sì com' (= sì come) = 'as/just as' or 'as soon as'; fui is the perfective past of essere ('I was' / 'I had become inside') and dentro = 'inside'. The whole phrase sets the time-frame for what follows.
segments.purgatorio-27-g63: This is a conditional/factual image introduced by in un bogliente vetro gittato mi sarei per rinfrescarmi: in + noun = 'into/in a'; bogliente (poetic form of 'bollente') = 'boiling, seething'; vetro literally 'glass' (here a metaphorical basin/surface); gittato mi sarei = reflexive conditional of gettare: 'I would have thrown myself' (gittato = past participle used with conditional 'sarei'); per rinfrescarmi = 'in order to refresh myself / to cool off.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g64: Tant' era ivi lo ’ncendio sanza metro is an exclamative statement: tant' era = 'so great/so strong was'; ivi = 'there'; lo ’ncendio = 'the fire/conflagration' (the apostrophe marks the dropped initial vowel); sanza metro = 'without measure' (sanza = without, metro = measure, i.e. beyond measure).
segments.purgatorio-27-g31: Credi per certo = an imperative or exhortation ('believe me / be sure'), with per certo as an adverbial phrase meaning 'for certain'. che introduces a subordinate clause. se ... stessi uses the imperfect subjunctive (stessi) in a customary Italian conditional protasis ('if you were to stay'), with dentro a l’alvo = 'inside the inmost part' (dentro + preposition a + the elided article l’). ben mille anni uses ben as an intensifier meaning 'a full' or 'well' a thousand years.
segments.purgatorio-27-g32: Non ti potrebbe far = main clause with potrebbe (conditional present of potere, 'could') + infinitive far ('make'). The clitic ti precedes the finite verb (non ti potrebbe) and is the object affected ('make you ...'). d’un capel calvo is an archaic compression of di un capello calvo = 'a single hair (on your head) bald'. The clause denies that even one hair could be made bald.
segments.purgatorio-27-g33: E se tu forse credi = 'and if you perhaps believe/think' — se + present indicative credi here expresses the supposition. ch’io t’inganni is a subordinate clause introduced by che with the present subjunctive inganni (ch’io inganni) meaning 'that I deceive you' (subjunctive used in dependent clauses after verbs of belief/doubt). The clitic t’ is the object pronoun 'you' attached to the verb inganni.
segments.purgatorio-27-g34: Fatti ver' lei: fatti is the reflexive/imperative form of farsi ('make yourself' / 'go' / 'get yourself'), an instruction to the addressee. ver' is an archaic contraction of verso (toward), so ver' lei = 'toward her/it' (the flame). The whole phrase is an imperative: 'go toward her.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g35: e fatti far credenza uses a causative construction: fatti (reflexive imperative of farsi) + far (infinitive) + credenza (noun) — 'cause belief to be made of you' i.e. 'make them believe you' or 'give proof.' con le tue mani al lembo d’i tuoi panni specifies the means: touch the hem of your garments with your hands (d’i = di + i, archaic elision). Word order places the means after the causative phrase.
segments.purgatorio-27-g36: Pon giù omai is an imperative command: pon (archaic imperative of porre/por) 'put' or 'set down', giù 'down', and omai 'now/by now' (temporal adverb). The short phrase is a brisk exhortation to discard worry.
segments.purgatorio-27-g37: pon giù ogne temenza repeats the imperative with ogne (archaic for ogni = 'every') and temenza ('fear'). The repetition pon giù intensifies the command and ogne temenza is the object to be set aside.
segments.purgatorio-27-g38: Volgiti in qua e vieni: volgiti is the reflexive imperative 'turn yourself' (turn around/face this way); in qua is an older locution meaning 'to here' or 'this way'; e vieni is an imperative 'and come' (vieni = 'come' as an address to 'you'). The sequence directs the addressee physically toward the speaker/place.
segments.purgatorio-27-g39: Entra sicuro is a final imperative: entra 'enter' and sicuro used adverbially or as an adjective of manner meaning 'safe/secure/confidently' — 'enter without fear' or 'enter safely/confidently.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g40: This segment is a single punctuation mark (period) ending the line/sentence. It marks the close of the utterance and a pause before the next sentence.
segments.purgatorio-27-g20: In su is a fronted adverbial phrase meaning 'up' or 'upward' (in + su). le man commesse is a noun phrase: le (definite article) + man (mano, 'hands') modified by the past participle commesse ('entrusted/committed') agreeing in gender and number. mi protesi is the first‑person passato remoto (protendersi → protesi) with the reflexive pronoun mi ('myself'), so literally 'I stretched myself forth' = 'I stretched out.' The word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (adverbial + object phrase + verb).
segments.purgatorio-27-g21: guardando and imaginando are gerunds (present participial/gerund forms) expressing simultaneous actions: 'looking' and 'imagining.' il foco is the object of guardando. e links the two gerunds; forte functions adverbially modifying imaginando ('vividly/strongly'). The final noun phrase umani corpi già veduti accesi contains two past participles: veduti ('seen') and accesi ('lit/aflame'), postpositive modifiers describing the bodies he imagines.
segments.purgatorio-27-g22: Volsersi is a past (passato remoto) pronominal verb form meaning 'turned' (they turned themselves). The sentence uses inversion: the verb appears before its logical subject le buone scorte ('the good escorts'), and verso me is the prepositional phrase 'toward me.' The reflexive/ pronominal nature (si) is part of the verb meaning 'turn (oneself) toward.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g23: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' Virgilio is the subject/name. mi disse uses passato remoto of dire ('he said') with mi as the indirect object pronoun 'to me' (disse a me). The clause introduces direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-27-g24: Figliuol mio is a vocative address: figliuol (archaic spelling of figlio) 'son/child' followed by the possessive mio. In Italian vocatives the possessive often follows the noun; punctuation (comma) marks direct address.
segments.purgatorio-27-g25: qui 'here' is a locative adverb. può is third‑person singular present of potere ('may/can'); esser is the infinitive of essere used after può to form an impersonal construction ('there may be'). tormento is the noun 'torment/pain' serving as the predicate noun: 'here there may be torment.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g26: ma 'but' introduces a contrast. non negates the following noun; morte ('death') is the noun contrasted with tormento: 'but not death.' The compact, elliptical phrasing (no verb repeated) is typical of direct speech in poetry.
segments.purgatorio-27-g27: Ricorditi is a second-person singular imperative with the enclitic pronoun -ti; in modern Italian one would expect ricordati. It means 'remember (yourself)' or simply 'remember.' The verb is repeated in the next segment for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-27-g28: A second identical imperative for forceful repetition (double vocative/command), same form and meaning as the previous segmento — emphatic 'remember!'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g29: The clause begins with the coordinating e 'and' plus the conditional marker se 'if.' 'Io' is the subject 'I.' 'Sovresso' is an adverbial/prepositional adverb meaning 'upon/over' (i.e., on the back of Geryon). 'Gerïon' is the proper name. 'Ti' is the direct object pronoun 'you' placed before the verb. 'Guidai' is the passato remoto (first-person singular) of guidare: 'I guided' or 'I brought.' 'Salvo' is an adjective meaning 'safe,' used predicatively after the verb ('I guided you safe' = 'I brought you to safety').
segments.purgatorio-27-g30: The interrogative 'che' here = 'what' introduces the rhetorical question. 'Farò' is the future tense (first-person singular) of fare: 'what will I do.' 'Ora' = 'now.' 'Presso più a Dio' is a compact poetic phrase: presso = 'near' (with a following a + Dio), più = 'more' (comparative), so the phrase asks 'what will I do now that will bring me nearer to God?' — 'presso' is used idiomatically to indicate proximity in relation to God.
segments.purgatorio-27-g65: Lo is the masculine definite article (poetic lo instead of il) placed before the noun phrase; dolce is an adjective qualifying padre and follows the article in the normal Italian order 'article + adjective + noun'; mio is the possessive adjective 'my' placed after the noun in a familiar/poetic style.
segments.purgatorio-27-g66: per + infinitive (confortarmi) expresses purpose: 'in order to comfort me'; the clitic -mi is attached to the infinitive and marks the direct object 'me'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g67: pur here means 'still/indeed' (a concessive/adverbial use); di introduces the topic 'of Beatrice' (i.e. speaking about her); ragionando is a gerund ('reasoning/talking') forming a circumstantial clause that links to the imperfect andato andava (literally 'was going about'), which here functions idiomatically: andava + gerund = 'kept on doing' (he kept talking).
segments.purgatorio-27-g68: dicendo is a gerund ('saying') that introduces the literal words which follow; it links the speech-reporting verb to the quotation and shows an action simultaneous with andava.
segments.purgatorio-27-g69: This is the quoted utterance. Li is the (poetic) plural definite article 'the' before occhi ('eyes'); suoi is the possessive agreeing in number/gender with occhi and refers back to Beatrice; già is an adverb 'already'; veder is an infinitive used with parmi (a compound verbal expression pari + mi contracted to parmi) meaning 'it seems to me to see' — Parmi literally 'seems to me', so 'già veder parmi' = 'I already seem to see'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g70: Guidavaci is the imperfect tense of guidare with the enclitic object -ci 'us' attached: 'was guiding us' (continuous/past habitual); una voce is the subject 'a voice'; che introduces a relative clause; cantava is imperfect 'was singing'; di là is a locative adverbial 'from there/over there' placed after cantava.
segments.purgatorio-27-g71: A simple coordinate 'and we': e = 'and'; noi = subject pronoun 'we' (stressed by position and coordination).
segments.purgatorio-27-g72: attenti is an adjective (past participle used adjectivally) meaning 'attentive'; pur again = 'still/still so'; a lei = prepositional phrase 'to it/to her' (referring back to 'voce'), with lei used as a feminine object pronoun.
segments.purgatorio-27-g73: venimmo is the passato remoto of venire 'we came/reached'; fuor (poetic for fuori) is an adverb 'out'; là 'there'; ove 'where' introduces a clause; si montava is reflexive/imperfect 'it rose/was mounting' — si here makes the verb intransitive/passive-like (the path or way 'mounts' up).
segments.purgatorio-27-g74: Venite is the vocative/imperative plural 'come!' (here reported speech), an address to listeners; in quotations it preserves the imperative force.
segments.purgatorio-27-g75: This is a Latin phrase inside the Italian: benedicti is Latin nominative plural 'blessed'; Patris mei is genitive 'of my Father' (mei = 'my'); the three words function as the addressed group 'blessed (ones) of my Father'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g76: sonò is passato remoto of sonare 'it sounded/resounded'; dentro a un lume = 'within/inside a light' (dentro + a + noun is idiomatic here); che lì era is a relative clause 'that was there', with era simple past 'was'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g77: tal che is a fixed conjunction meaning 'such that/so that'; mi is the indirect/direct object 'me'; vinse is passato remoto of vincere 'overcame' (it overcame/overpowered me); e links the second clause; guardar is the infinitive 'to look' with nol (poetic contraction of 'non lo' = 'it not') preceding the auxiliary; potei is passato remoto 'I could' (here negative: 'I could not look').
segments.purgatorio-27-g78: Lo sol is a poetic order: lo (definite article) + sol (poetic form of sole, 'the sun'); va is present indicative 'goes/is going' — straightforward clause 'the sun goes'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g79: soggiunse is passato remoto of soggiungere 'he/she added', a reporting verb that frames the following quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-27-g80: A short clause in the quotation: e = 'and'; vien is present indicative (viene contracted) 'comes'; la sera 'the evening' (la + sera).
segments.purgatorio-27-g81: non + v' (contracted vi) + arrestate is the negative imperative 'do not stop/stay' addressed to you (plural); v' (= vi) is the clitic indicating location/second-person object depending on register, here 'there'/'yourselves' tied to arrestate.
segments.purgatorio-27-g82: ma = 'but'; studiate is the (plural) imperative of studiare 'consider/mind/attend to' (an exhortation); il passo = 'the step/the passage' — a noun object which here means the footing or the passage to be taken.
segments.purgatorio-27-g83: mentre che = 'while/as long as'; l'occidente = 'the west/the region of sunset' (the western sky); non si annera is a reflexive/future-oriented clause: annera (from annerare 'to blacken') in present or future sense here 'does not yet darken' with si as reflexive/impersonal marker — i.e. 'while the west has not become dark.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g1: Sì come quando introduces a simile: 'Just as when...'. i primi raggi vibra: vibra is 3rd-person singular present (poetic/elliptical agreement) meaning 'tremble' or 'quiver' and governs 'i primi raggi' (the subject). là dove ... sparse is a relative clause giving the place: 'where his maker scattered the blood'; fattor is an archaic form for 'facer'/'maker'. The word order (verb after subject in vibra) is normal in elevated/poetic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-27-g2: cadendo is a present participle ('falling') that describes the motion of Ibero; the participle phrase 'cadendo Ibero' modifies the prior place, i.e. 'where the Ebro, falling under the high Libra...'. Ibero here is a proper name (the river Ebro) — the supplied senses list doesn't include a proper-name sense, so that is marked separately below. l’alta Libra: Libra is used as a celestial sign/constellation ('high Libra').
segments.purgatorio-27-g3: This coordinate clause continues the descriptive image: e l’onde in Gange da nona rïarse. da nona indicates 'from the ninth (hour)', rïarse is a rare/poetic form meaning 'to be dried/parched' — here the waves of the Ganges are said to become dried from the ninth hour. Word order is standard: subject (le onde) + circumstantial phrases + verb (implied by rïarse).
segments.purgatorio-27-g4: sì stava il sole: 'sì' (= so/thus) signals the comparison's result; stava is imperfect (or historical present used poetically) of stare meaning 'stood' or 'was positioned' — 'the sun stood thus'. The order is adverb + verb + subject, a common poetic inversion to match meter.
segments.purgatorio-27-g5: onde here means 'whence' / 'from which' and introduces the consequence: 'whence the day was passing away' — sen giva is the poetic form of 'se ne andava' (se + ne + andava) meaning 'was going away' or 'was departing'. Giva is an older spelling of giava/andava.
segments.purgatorio-27-g6: come introduces a further simile: 'as the angel of God joyful appeared to us.' apparse is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of apparire, 'appeared.' ci is the indirect object 'to us'; lieto modifies 'angel' and agrees in meaning 'joyful'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g7: Fuor de la fiamma = 'outside the flame'; fuor (poetic for fuori) + de (di) marks location 'outside of'. stava in su la riva: stava = 'stood' (imperfect/historical present), in su is an emphatic/localizing prepositional phrase meaning 'upon', and riva = 'bank/shore'. The order places the locative phrase before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.purgatorio-27-g8: e cantava is imperfect 'and he was singing' (3rd-singular), introducing the direct quotation. The quoted words are a liturgical/LATIN beatitude; here they function as direct speech. Note cantava is descriptive/past tense, not a present narration.
segments.purgatorio-27-g9: in voce = 'in a voice' (instrumental/expressive use of in with a noun). assai più che = 'much more than' (assai = very/much, più che = more than) forming a comparative; la nostra viva = 'our living (voice)' with viva used adjectivally/nominally to mean 'living one' or 'living voice'. The clause describes the quality of the singing.
segments.purgatorio-27-g10: Poscia = 'afterward/then.' «Più non si va» is an impersonal construction with si + 3rd-singular verb: 'one goes no further' / 'it is no longer possible to go on.' Here Più non = 'no longer', si va = impersonal 'one goes' or 'one proceeds'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g11: se pria non morde: se = 'if' (introducing the condition), pria = archaic/adverbial 'before/first', non morde = 'the fire does not bite' (morde is present tense of mordere, here used in the sense 'to bite/sting/afflict'). Together it means 'unless first [the fire] bite' (i.e. unless the fire first affects you).
segments.purgatorio-27-g12: anime sante is the address 'holy souls' (anima in the vocative/accusative plural, sante agreeing). This is the addressee of the injunction.
segments.purgatorio-27-g13: il foco: the direct object 'the fire.' The colon in the original marks the end of that clause and the beginning of the imperative that follows.
segments.purgatorio-27-g14: intrate in esso is a 2nd-person plural imperative (intrate = 'enter!') followed by the prepositional phrase 'in esso' ('into it'). The imperative form addresses the 'anime sante' just named.
segments.purgatorio-27-g15: e al cantar di là non siate sorde: 'and to the singing over there be not deaf.' non siate is present subjunctive (2nd pl.) used here as a prohibitive/admonition ('do not be'); sorde = 'deaf' (feminine plural agreeing with 'anime'). al cantar di là = 'to the singing over there' (al = a + il).
segments.purgatorio-27-g16: ci disse come noi li fummo presso: ci = 'to us', disse = passato remoto 'he said', come noi li fummo presso = 'as we were near him/them' (fummo presso = 'we were close'). The clause reports that he spoke these words as they approached.
segments.purgatorio-27-g17: per ch’io divenni tal: per ch' = per che = 'wherefore/therefore', io divenni = passato remoto 'I became', tal = 'such' (i.e. in the state described). This gives the narrator's reaction to hearing the words.
segments.purgatorio-27-g18: quando lo ’ntesi: quando = 'when'; lo = direct object pronoun 'it' (referring to the words); ’ntesi is an elided/poetic form of intesi (passato remoto of intendere/intendere/hear) meaning 'I heard it' or 'I perceived it.' This gives the temporal clause 'when I heard it.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g19: qual è colui che ne la fossa è messo: qual è colui che = 'such as is he who'; ne la fossa = 'in the pit/ditch of it' (ne = 'in/therein'), è messo = 'is placed' (passive sense, messo = past participle of mettere). The simile compares the speaker's state to someone set down in a pit.
segments.purgatorio-27-g143: Tanto voler: the infinitive voler is used substantively (a common medieval/poetic usage) meaning 'so great a desire' or 'such eagerness.' sopra voler: a prepositional phrase 'on/above desire,' i.e. an additional desire ('desire upon desire'). mi venne: venne is a past simple (passato remoto-like narrative past) and mi is the dative/indirect object 'to me' — 'came to me' or 'overtook me.' de l’esser sù: de (archaic di) = 'from,' l’esser is the nominalized infinitive 'the being' and sù = 'up/aloft,' so the whole phrase gives the source or cause: 'from being up there.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g144: ch’: enclitic che 'that,' introducing the result clause. ad ogne passo poi: a = 'at,' ogne (archaic ogn-) 'every,' passo 'step,' poi 'afterward' — a temporal phrase 'at every step thereafter.' al volo: a + il 'to the/for the' + volo 'flight,' meaning 'toward flight' or 'for flying.' mi sentia: sentia is the imperfect of sentire (I felt / I was feeling) and mi is dative indicating the feeling occurs to the speaker ('I felt on me'); the imperfect gives a continuing or repeated sensation. crecer le penne: crecer is the infinitive complement after the perception verb sentia ('felt [them] grow'); le penne 'the feathers' (here figuratively the wing-feathers), so 'I felt my feathers growing.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g145: 'Come' here means 'when/as'; the phrase 'la scala tutta sotto noi' is the noun phrase 'the entire stairway beneath us'; 'fu corsa' is passato remoto compound-like use (literally 'was run' or 'had been run through') with 'corsa' as past participle of correre; 'e fummo' is passato remoto 'and we were'; 'in su ’l grado superno' places the action 'upon the upper step' (superno = heavenly/upper). The line uses poetic, slightly archaic word order.
segments.purgatorio-27-g146: Inversion for emphasis: the verb phrase 'in me ficcò' (ficcò = passato remoto of ficcare, 'he fixed/ thrust') takes the indirect/locative 'in me' ('at me' / 'into me'), then the subject 'Virgilio', and finally the object 'li occhi suoi' ('his eyes'); 'li' is the archaic article and the possessive 'suoi' follows the noun (poetic order).
segments.purgatorio-27-g147: 'e disse' is simply the conjunction plus the passato remoto verb 'disse' (3rd sg. of dire) introducing direct speech; in English, 'and (he) said:'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g148: The quoted subject 'Il temporal foco e l’etterno' names two things ('the temporal fire and the eternal [fire]'); 'veduto hai' is the compound perfect formed with past participle 'veduto' + present auxiliary 'hai' (2nd sg.), here meaning 'you have seen'—the participle before the auxiliary is a poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-27-g149: A single vocative 'figlio' addressing the speaker ('my son'); the punctuation marks direct address.
segments.purgatorio-27-g150: 'e se’ venuto' is 'and you have come' with archaic contraction 'se’' for 'sei' (2nd sg. of essere used with venuto); 'in parte' = 'to the point/place'; the relative clause 'dov’ io per me più oltre non discerno' = 'where I, for my part, can no longer discern further'—'discerno' is present indicative 'I discern', and 'più oltre' = 'any further'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g151: 'Tratto t’ho' is an archaic inversion of the passato prossimo 'ti ho tratto' ('I have brought/drawn you'); the pronoun 't’' is clitic attached to the auxiliary 'ho'; 'qui' = 'here'; 'con ingegno e con arte' specifies the means ('with ingenuity and with skill').
segments.purgatorio-27-g152: 'lo tuo piacere' uses the definite article before the possessive (a common older/poetic usage) to mean 'your pleasure'; 'omai' = 'now/from now on'; 'prendi' is 2nd person singular imperative ('take' as a command or strong instruction addressed to 'you'); 'per duce' means 'as (your) guide/leader'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g153: 'fuor se’ de l’erte vie' = 'you are outside/off the steep ways': 'fuor' = 'out' (or 'outside'); 'se’' = contraction of 'sei' (you are); 'de' = 'of/from'; 'l’erte vie' = 'the steep ways' (erte = steep, agreeing with feminine plural 'vie'). This is a present-stative assertion about the pilgrim's position.
segments.purgatorio-27-g154: Parallel short clause 'fuor se’ de l’arte' repeats the same structure: 'you are outside of art/skill' (i.e., beyond the need for skill or beyond the realm of learned art). The terse repetition is poetic.
segments.purgatorio-27-g155: Vedi is the 2nd-person singular imperative (or a hortative present) meaning “behold/see.” Lo sol is an archaic article + noun construction; lo is the definite article placed before sol (the sun), and che introduces a relative clause modifying lo sol. ’n is an elided form of in (common in Dante), so che ’n fronte ti riluce = “that in/on (your) brow gleams.” Ti is a dative/locative pronoun indicating the person on whose brow the sun shines, and riluce is 3rd-person singular present indicative of rilucere (“gleams/shines”).
segments.purgatorio-27-g156: Again the verb vedi is an imperative addressing the addressee: “see/behold.” L’erbette is the contracted definite article + noun (here the article has elided before a vowel); erbette is a plural diminutive meaning small herbs/greens and functions as the direct object of vedi.
segments.purgatorio-27-g157: The phrase lists two coordinated nouns with definite articles (i fiori e li arbuscelli = “the flowers and the little shrubs”), where li is an older/dialectal plural article. Che is a relative pronoun introducing the clause that modifies the plants. Qui is an adverb “here”; la terra is the subject “the earth”; sol (here from solo) is an adverb “only” or “alone” emphasizing that the earth itself, not external agency, is the cause; da sé is a reflexive/complement meaning “of itself” or “by itself”; produce is 3rd-person singular present indicative “produces.” The overall clause means “which here the earth produces by itself.”
segments.purgatorio-27-g127: Ell' is the contracted form of ella ('she'). The phrase è ... vaga here uses the adjective vaga (from vagare) with an infinitive complement: 'essere vaga di + infinitive' = 'to be eager/pleased to [do/see]'. D'i suoi belli occhi = di + il (here contracted to d' + i) is a prepositional phrase 'with/of her beautiful eyes' specifying the manner of seeing; com' io de l'addornarmi con le mani is a comparison ('as I [am] about/for adorning myself with my hands'), where addornarmi is the infinitive addornare + enclitic reflexive mi 'to adorn myself'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g128: This short clause is elliptical: lei is the stressed pronoun 'her / to her' (dative/stressed form), lo vedere is the infinitive vedere used as a noun with lo ('the seeing of it' / 'seeing it'). Together they mean roughly 'for her, the seeing of it' or simply 'her seeing it'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g129: E = 'and'. Me is the stressed/tonic object pronoun 'me' (rather than mi), placed before the noun phrase l'ovrare (the article + infinitive ovrare, 'the doing/working of it'); appaga is 3rd-person singular present 'satisfies/pleases'. The whole clause means 'and the doing of it pleases me' (i.e. '...satisfies me').
segments.purgatorio-27-g130: E già 'and already' sets the temporal frame. Per + plural article li + splendori antelucani = 'by/through the pre-dawn splendors/gleams'; antelucani is an adjective meaning 'before dawn' (poetic/archaic), and the older article li marks the plural.
segments.purgatorio-27-g131: This is a relative clause introduced by che 'which/that.' Tanto...più grati forms a comparative 'so/so much...more pleasing'; a’ pellegrin (a + plural elided article) means 'to the pilgrims.' Surgon is an older 3rd-person-plural form of sorgere/sorgono 'they rise/appear,' and grati is an adjective 'pleasing' agreeing with pellegrin.
segments.purgatorio-27-g132: Quanto here introduces a comparison clause ('inasmuch as' / 'to the extent that') linking the preceding idea to the following subordinate clause; it functions as the comparative marker 'as/than.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g133: Tornando is a present gerund/participle 'returning' or 'when they return,' functioning adverbially to set the time/condition for the comparison.
segments.purgatorio-27-g134: Albergan is the 3rd-person-plural present of albergare 'they lodge/harbor' (poetic inversion places the verb before its subject). Men = 'less' (comparative adverb), and lontani 'far away/distant' modifies what is lodged; together: 'they lodge less far away.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g135: Le tenebre 'the darkness' is the subject; fuggian is the older 3rd-person-plural form of fuggire (here a subjunctive/poetic present form used narratively) meaning 'flee' or 'were fleeing/fled.' Da tutti lati 'from every side' is a simple prepositional phrase of separation/direction.
segments.purgatorio-27-g136: E 'and' connects clauses; 'l sonno mio = il sonno mio 'my sleep' (article elided as l'). Con esse 'with them' has the feminine plural pronoun esse referring back to le tenebre, so 'and my sleep fled with them.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g137: Ond' is a contraction of onde 'whence/therefore/whereupon' linking cause to result. Io is the subject 'I.' Leva’mi is an older past-tense narrative form with clitic (mi) attached meaning 'I raised myself / I got up' (reflexive); the apostrophe marks an elision in the written form.
segments.purgatorio-27-g138: Veggendo is a gerund/participle 'seeing' that links to the main verb leva’mi (giving the reason or circumstance). I gran maestri 'the great masters' is the direct object of veggendo as the thing seen; già levati is a past participle phrase 'already risen/up' describing their state.
segments.purgatorio-27-g139: This is a relative clause modifying Quel dolce pome. The verb phrase is an idiomatic 'va cercando' ('goes about seeking' or 'is sought'), where cercando is a gerund 'seeking' and va is the auxiliary 'goes/continues' giving a progressive sense; word order is inverted for meter: 'che per tanti rami cercando va la cura de' mortali' = 'which the care/concern of mortals goes seeking through so many branches.' La cura de' mortali is the grammatical subject (la cura = the anxious care/effort of humans).
segments.purgatorio-27-g140: Oggi 'today' sets the time; porrà is future simple 3rd-person-singular of porre 'will put/bring/establish.' In pace is an idiom 'at peace / to rest'; le tue fami 'your hungers' (fami plural) is the direct object. The whole clause means 'will put your hungers to rest today.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g141: Subject + adverbial phrase + object + verb in a compact, slightly inverted order: 'Virgilio' is the subject; 'inverso me' is a locative/adverbial phrase meaning 'turned toward me' (a literary use of inverso); 'queste cotali parole' is the direct object ('these such words' = 'such words') with 'cotali' agreeing in number; 'usò' is passato remoto (3rd sg. of usare) meaning 'he employed/used/said'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g142: 'e mai non furo' is a negative perfect-stative statement in passato remoto ('and never were there'); 'strenne' is the plural subject ('gifts'); the relative clause 'che fosser di piacere a queste iguali' uses the imperfect subjunctive 'fosser' (archaic spelling of 'fossero') to say 'that could be' or 'that might be' equal in pleasure to 'these' (queste refers back to the words). 'di piacere' is a noun phrase 'in respect of pleasure'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g106: Literary inversion and archaic spacing: 'Poco' (an adverb) opens the clause and modifies the impersonal verbal phrase 'parer potea' (lit. 'could appear'). 'Poter + parer' here means 'could be seen/appear'; 'lì' marks location ('there'). The phrase 'del di fori' literally 'of the day outside' (del = di + il; di = dì 'day'; fori = 'outside') describes the daylight exterior to the place.
segments.purgatorio-27-g107: 'ma' is the simple coordinating conjunction 'but', introducing the contrast with what follows.
segments.purgatorio-27-g108: 'per quel poco' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of/with that little' or 'from that small amount (of light)'; 'per' here indicates cause or measure, 'quel' is the demonstrative 'that', and 'poco' is the noun/adverb 'little'. The commas mark it off as a circumstantial phrase.
segments.purgatorio-27-g109: 'vedea' is an archaic imperfect form of vedere ('I could / I saw'); the subject pronoun 'io' is emphatic and follows the verb (inverted order). 'Le stelle' is the direct object. 'di lor solere' is a genitive/partitive phrase: 'of their wont' or 'that they are accustomed to' (solere used substantively/infinitival in sense of custom). The final 'e più chiare e maggiori' is a comparative phrase ('and [were] more bright and greater/larger'), with 'più' strengthening the adjectives 'chiare' and 'maggiori'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g110: Two gerunds joined by 'e' describe simultaneous actions: 'ruminando' (literally 'chewing' but here 'musing, pondering') and 'mirando' ('gazing'). The duplicated 'Sì ... sì' is emphatic 'so / thus' and links the manner of the action; 'in quelle' = 'at/on those (things)', here referring back to the stars.
segments.purgatorio-27-g111: 'mi prese il sonno' is a common idiom: 'prese' (past of prendere) takes 'mi' (me) as indirect object and 'il sonno' as subject/thing that seizes — idiomatically 'sleep seized me' = 'I fell asleep'. The verb 'prese' is a simple past/preterite form used narratively.
segments.purgatorio-27-g112: A resumptive or appositive phrase: 'il sonno che sovente' begins a relative clause ('sleep which often ...'). 'Il sonno' is repeated to introduce the defining relative, and 'sovente' ('often') modifies the relative verb that follows.
segments.purgatorio-27-g113: 'anzi che' here functions temporally as 'before'. It is followed by the clause '’l fatto sia' where 'l' = 'the' and 'fatto' = 'deed/what is to happen', while 'sia' is the present subjunctive of essere used in a temporal subordinate ('before the deed be/done' = 'before it happens').
segments.purgatorio-27-g114: 'sa le novelle' is a simple clause: 'sa' (present of sapere) takes 'le novelle' ('the news/tidings') as direct object — 'sleep knows the news'. In context the meaning is proverbial: sleep often 'knows' or anticipates news before events fully occur.
segments.purgatorio-27-g115: Ne l'ora = 'in/at the hour' (ne here functioning as 'in' or 'in that'); the phrase sets the precise time of the vision. The article and noun l'ora = 'the hour.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g116: Credo is the speaker's comment 'I believe / I think' and qualifies the following clause as Dante's recollection rather than assertive fact.
segments.purgatorio-27-g117: Che de l'orïente prima raggiò nel monte Citerea = 'when/from the East first shone upon Mount Cytherea': che introduces the temporal clause; de = 'from/of' in older orthography; prima = 'first'; raggiò = past of raggiare 'shone'; nel monte Citerea names the location (Citerea = Cytherea, an epithet of Venus).
segments.purgatorio-27-g118: Che di foco d'amor par sempre ardente is a relative clause describing Citerea: 'which, of the fire of love, seems always burning' (par = 'seems'; ardente = 'burning/ardent').
segments.purgatorio-27-g119: Giovane e bella in sogno mi parea donna vedere andar per una landa cogliendo fiori is a slightly inverted descriptive clause: mi parea = 'seemed to me' (dative of feeling), and the infinitive construction donna vedere andar ... = 'to see a woman walking across a plain picking flowers'; in sogno = 'in a dream'. The present participle cogliendo ('gathering') describes the action while she walks.
segments.purgatorio-27-g120: E cantando dicea = 'and, singing, she said' — cantando is the present participle 'singing' that sets the manner, dicea is the imperfect 'she was saying' / 'she said' used in narrative.
segments.purgatorio-27-g121: «Sappia qualunque il mio nome dimanda ch’i’ mi son Lia: Sappia is a present subjunctive used optatively/imperatively — 'let (him) know'; qualunque il mio nome dimanda = 'whoever asks my name' (qualunque = 'whichever/ whoever'); ch’i’ mi son Lia = 'that I am Leah' (ch' = che; i' = io contracted; mi son = 'I am').
segments.purgatorio-27-g122: E vo movendo intorno le belle mani a farmi una ghirlanda = 'and I go moving my fair hands about to make myself a garland': vo (from andare) = 'I go / I move about', movendo = 'moving' (participle), intorno = 'around', a farmi = 'to make for myself' (farmi = fare + mi, infinitive with clitic), ghirlanda = 'garland.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g123: Per here expresses purpose ('in order to' / 'to') and governs the infinitive phrase that follows. Piacermi is the verb piacere with the clitic mi attached, literally 'to please me' (i.e. 'to give me pleasure' / 'for my pleasure'); a lo specchio is a prepositional phrase 'at/in the mirror' with the older article lo.
segments.purgatorio-27-g124: Qui 'here' localises the action. M’ (mi) is the reflexive/clitic pronoun tied to the verb; addorno is 1st person singular present indicative (older form) meaning 'I adorn (myself)' or 'I dress/prepare myself.' The clause is short and idiomatic: 'here I adorn myself.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g125: Ma 'but' introduces contrast. Mia suora Rachel is 'my sister Rachel' (suora here as 'sister' in the familiar/poetic sense, not necessarily a nun). Mai non si smaga is a negative construction where mai non = 'never'; si is the reflexive pronoun; smaga (from smagare) means to free/unbind from an enchantment or spell—so 'never frees herself.' Dal suo miraglio 'from her miraglio' (miraglio = marvel/wonder/enchantment) indicates what she is not freed from.
segments.purgatorio-27-g126: E 'and' links the clause; siede is 3rd person singular present indicative of sedere, 'she sits' or 'is seated.' Tutto giorno is an adverbial phrase 'all day' (tutto used adverbially + giorno). The phrase states a habitual or continuing action.
segments.purgatorio-27-g84: Dritta is an adjective (feminine) placed before the verb and used adverbially to qualify la via (the feminine noun 'via'), so: 'straight.' Salia is an archaic/poetic third-person singular past-tense form of salire (to climb/ascend) — 'went up' or 'climbed.' The phrase per entro ’l sasso is a prepositional phrase (‘through/within the rock’), with ’l as the contracted article il. Verso tal parte begins a direction phrase (‘toward that side’). Ch’ introduces a relative clause; io is the subject of toglieva (imperfect ‘I was taking away/preventing’), with i raggi dinanzi a me as its direct object phrase (‘the rays in front of me’) and del sol specifying whose rays. The final ch’era già basso is another relative clause (che = which) with era (imperfect of essere) and già basso = 'already low,' describing the sun.
segments.purgatorio-27-g85: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' Di pochi scaglion is an ablative/measure expression meaning 'by a few steps/stairs' (di + quantity). Levammo is passato remoto, first-person plural of levare ('we raised' / here idiomatically 'we quickened/raised our step'), and i saggi is the direct object or appositive 'the sages/wise ones' (in context, Dante's companions).
segments.purgatorio-27-g86: Che introduces an explanatory/relative clause. ’l sol is the sun (’l = il), and corcar is an infinitive meaning 'to lie down' (a poetic verb for going to rest) — here referring to the sun's setting; the clause is terse/elliptical because Dante uses corcar to name the event (the sun going to bed).
segments.purgatorio-27-g87: Per here means 'because of' / 'on account of.' L’ombra is 'the shadow,' and che si spense is a relative clause: che = which/that; si is reflexive; spense is passato remoto of spegnere ('it extinguished' / 'fell away'), so 'because of the shadow that was extinguished/that fell.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g88: Sentimmo is passato remoto first-person plural of sentire ('we perceived/heard'). Dietro here means 'behind' (i.e. behind us). The final sequence e io e li miei saggi repeats subjects for emphasis ('both I and my wise companions'); word order places the perception (sentimmo) before the explicit agents.
segments.purgatorio-27-g89: E = and; pria che = 'before' (a temporal conjunction that normally takes the subjunctive). ’n is the contraction of in. Tutte le sue parti immense = 'all its immense parts' (sue agrees with the thing referred to, here 'horizon'). Fosse is imperfect subjunctive of essere (fosse) used after pria che to indicate the state that had not yet come to be. Orizzonte fatto d’uno aspetto is a participial phrase 'horizon made into one appearance' (fatto = past participle of fare).
segments.purgatorio-27-g90: E = and; notte is the subject 'night.' Avesse is imperfect subjunctive of avere (parallel sequence with fosse) meaning 'had.' Tutte sue dispense = 'all its dispensations/provisions' (dispense used metaphorically for what night 'brings' or 'provides'); the clause says night had taken on all its accommodations/resources.
segments.purgatorio-27-g91: Ciascun di noi = 'each one of us.' D’un grado is a locution 'on a step' (grado here = step/stage). Fece is passato remoto of fare ('made'), and letto here means 'bed' (each made a bed on his step) — note that letto in modern dictionaries can be ambiguous (read/bed); context requires 'bed.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g92: Ché = 'for/because.' La natura del monte = 'the nature/disposition of the mountain.' Ci is the indirect object 'to us' / 'from us.' Affranse is a poetically used verb meaning that the mountain deprived or cut off something from them (Dante's verb is unusual/archaic here). La possa del salir più = 'the power/ability to climb any further' (possa = power/ability, here a substantive), e ’l diletto = 'and the pleasure' — both objects of affranse (the mountain's nature took them away from the travelers).
segments.purgatorio-27-g93: Quali introduces a simile ('such as' / 'like'), followed by a reflexive progressive construction si stanno ruminando = 'are chewing the cud' (stanno + gerund ruminando). Manse is a postpositive noun meaning their mountain-holdings/folds; the subject le capre is placed after for poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-27-g94: State rapide e proterve is a simple predicate: state (present of stare) = 'are' (here describing condition), rapide e proterve = 'swift and headstrong/insolent'. Sovra le cime avante = 'on the forward peaks' (avante here = 'before/forward'), che sien pranse is a relative clause with subjunctive 'sien' ('which may be / which are') and pranse = 'have had their midday meal' (pranzo), i.e. 'before they have fed.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g95: Tacite = feminine plural adjective agreeing with le capre ('silent'); a l'ombra = 'in the shade'. The phrase describes their quiet posture while resting.
segments.purgatorio-27-g96: Mentre che = 'while'; ’l sol ferve = 'the sun boils' (ferve = 'seethes/boils', a vivid, figurative present). This temporal clause explains why the goats are in the shade.
segments.purgatorio-27-g97: Guardate dal pastor: guardate here is a past participle/adjectival use 'watched' or 'kept' (they are 'watched by the shepherd'), dal = 'by the' and pastor = 'shepherd'.
segments.purgatorio-27-g98: che ’n su la verga poggiato s’è = relative clause 'who, leaning on his staff, is' (poggiato = past participle 'having leaned'; s’è = 'he is' / 'has himself leaned'); e lor di posa serve = 'and serves them for rest' i.e. 'and gives them a pause/relief' (serve = 'is of use/serves'). Note the archaic contracted n (= in) before su.
segments.purgatorio-27-g99: E quale il mandrïan che fori alberga = 'and like the herdsman who shelters outside' (mandrïan = herdsman; fori = 'outside', alberga = 'lodges/dwells'). The comparative particle quale again introduces a parallel image.
segments.purgatorio-27-g100: Lungo il pecuglio suo queto pernotta = 'along his flock he quietly spends the night' (lungo = 'along'; pecuglio here = his flock/herd; queto = 'quiet'; pernotta = 'spends the night' — present simple narrative).
segments.purgatorio-27-g101: Guardando = present participle 'watching' or 'keeping guard'; perché = 'so that / lest' introducing a purpose/fear clause; fiera = 'wild beast'; non lo sperga = 'does not scatter it' (sperga = subjunctive/present meaning 'scatter/disperse'), so the herdsman watches to prevent beasts from scattering the flock.
segments.purgatorio-27-g102: Tali eravamo tutti e tre allotta = 'Such were all three of us then' (tali = 'such'; eravamo = imperfect 'we were'; tutti e tre = 'all three'; allotta = 'at that time/then'). The clause summarizes the simile.
segments.purgatorio-27-g103: Io come capra = 'I like a goat' (io for emphasis; come = 'as/like'; capra = 'goat'), a straightforward simile identifying the speaker with a goat's behavior.
segments.purgatorio-27-g104: Ed ei come pastori = 'and he like the shepherds' (ed = 'and'; ei = archaic subject pronoun 'he'; come = 'as/like'; pastori = plural 'shepherds' used in the simile).
segments.purgatorio-27-g105: Fasciati quinci e quindi d’alta grotta = 'wrapped/bandaged on this side and that by the high grotto' (fasciati = past participle 'wrapped/ bound'; quinci e quindi = 'on this side and on that side'; d'alta grotta = 'of the high cave/grotto', indicating how they were enveloped).
segments.purgatorio-27-g41: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this line to what precedes. Io is the stressed subject pronoun 'I'. Pur (an archaic/poetic form of pure/pur) functions as an adverb meaning 'still' / 'nevertheless' and modifies the predicative adjective that follows. Fermo is an adjective used predicatively ('standing firm' / 'resolved'), and the second e simply links the adjective phrase to the following prepositional phrase. Contra is a preposition meaning 'against' and takes the noun coscïenza ('conscience', with the diaeresis marking syllabic i) as its object. The whole clause is elliptical: 'And I (was) still firm and against conscience.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g42: Quando introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). Mi is the unstressed object pronoun 'me' placed before the verb. Vide is the passato remoto (simple past) 'he saw'. Star is the infinitive (stare) used after vedere to indicate seeing someone in a state ('saw me be/stand ...'). Pur again means 'still/indeed', and fermo e duro are two adjectival complements describing the state in which he saw the speaker; duro here means 'hard' or 'stubborn'. The word order (object pronoun + verb + infinitive + complements) is normal after vedere + infinitive.
segments.purgatorio-27-g43: Turbato is a past participle used predicatively ('disturbed') describing the speaker's state when he spoke. Un poco ('a little') is an adverbial quantifier placed between the participle and the verb. Disse is the passato remoto 'he said'; the subject (the guide) is omitted because it's clear from context.
segments.purgatorio-27-g44: Or is an adverb meaning 'now' or 'at this point'. Vedi is here an imperative/imperatival expression addressed to Dante ('look' or 'see'), formed like the present indicative 2nd singular but functioning as an exhortation in direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-27-g45: Figlio is a vocative noun ('son') addressing Dante directly; Italian uses the uninflected nomitive form as a direct address (no separate vocative case).
segments.purgatorio-27-g46: Tra is the preposition 'between'. The coordinate phrase 'Bëatrice e te' ('Beatrice and you') is the complement of the preposition; te is the tonic form required after a preposition (not the subject tu). È is the present indicative of essere 'is', and questo muro is the nominative (or presentational) phrase 'this wall' functioning as the thing that stands between them. Word order places the prepositional phrase before the copula and its predicate.
segments.purgatorio-27-g47: Come is the subordinating conjunction 'as' introducing a simile. Al nome di Tisbe ('at the name of Thisbe') is a prepositional phrase modifying the action. Aperse is passato remoto 'opened' and takes il ciglio ('the eyelid/eyelash' = 'eye') as its direct object. Piramo (Pyramus) is the grammatical subject but placed after the object for poetic inversion (object–subject order). In su la morte is an adverbial phrase 'up/toward death' specifying the direction of his gaze.
segments.purgatorio-27-g48: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Riguardolla is a poetic/archaic form where the verb riguardare ('to look at/regard') is combined with the enclitic object pronoun la ('her') appended: 'riguardò + la' → riguardolla, meaning 'he looked back at her'. The form is compressed for metrical reasons but functions as a single past-tense verb with clitic.
segments.purgatorio-27-g49: Allor che introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ’L is the elided form of il ('the') before gelso (mulberry tree). Diventò is passato remoto 'became/turned into'. Vermiglio is an adjective 'vermilion' or 'crimson' describing the color change of the mulberry.
segments.purgatorio-27-g50: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so', signalling the drawing of the simile to the speaker's own reaction.
segments.purgatorio-27-g51: La mia durezza is the noun phrase 'my hardness' (the speaker's hardness or stubbornness). Fatta is the past participle of fare used here in a passive/resultant sense ('having been made' or 'made into'). Solla is an archaic/poetic adjective (spelling/usage differs from modern Italian); in context it functions as a predicative complement to fatta. (In meaning it signals that Dante's hardness has been changed—the precise archaic nuance is lexical/semantic rather than a different grammatical construction.)
segments.purgatorio-27-g52: Mi volsi is the passato remoto first-person singular reflexive 'I turned (myself)'—here 'I turned (my gaze/attention)'. Al is the contraction of a + il 'to the', and savio duca is the noun phrase 'wise duke' (duca here meaning 'guide'—Virgil). The reflexive mi plus volsi marks Dante's physical/attentive turning toward his guide.
segments.purgatorio-27-g53: Udendo is the gerund of udere/udire ('on hearing' or 'having heard') and supplies the circumstance/cause for the main action (i.e., 'upon hearing the name'). Il nome is the direct object 'the name'. Che introduces a relative clause describing that name. Ne la mente uses the pronoun ne with la mente ('in the mind' / 'in my mind'); ne here functions as a locative/partitive element linked to mente. Sempre is the adverb 'always' and mi is the indirect/reflexive pronoun 'to me'; rampolla (from rampollare) is present tense 'sprouts up' or 'puts forth new shoots'—figuratively, 'that ever sprouts up in my mind.'
segments.purgatorio-27-g54: Ond’ is an elided/poetic form of 'onde' or 'ond', functioning as a connective adverb meaning 'whereupon' / 'therefore' / 'and so'. Ei is an archaic third-person pronoun 'he'. Crollò is passato remoto third-person singular of crollare 'he shook/collapsed/trembled'; here it applies to la fronte ('the brow/forehead'), meaning he moved or shook his brow in reaction. E disse is 'and said'—the verb introducing direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-27-g55: Come here is an exclamatory particle ('How!' / 'What!') used as the opening of direct speech; grammatically it is the interrogative/adverb 'how' used as an interjection. In context it begins the duke's exclamation.
segments.purgatorio-27-g158: Mentre che is a temporal conjunction pair meaning “while” (mentre + the relative/connector che). Veñan (vegnan) is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person plural present form of venire meaning “they come/are coming.” Li occhi belli is the noun phrase with the plural article li + noun occhi followed by the adjective belli (adjective after noun is normal in Dante); the final che begins the relative clause modifying occhi.
segments.purgatorio-27-g159: Lagrimando is a present participle / gerund meaning “weeping,” used adverbially inside the relative clause to describe the manner of the eyes’ action.
segments.purgatorio-27-g160: A te venir is an infinitive phrase literally “to come to you”; the preposition a with the infinitive expresses motion/toward. Mi fenno is the 3rd-person plural passato (remote) of fare with the clitic mi: literally “they made/caused me” (i.e. they caused me to come). Word order is poetic: the infinitive + clitic + past verb express what the eyes did.
segments.purgatorio-27-g161: Seder ti puoi uses the infinitive seder (sedere) followed by the clitic ti and the modal verb puoi (you can) — Dante prefers placing the infinitive before the personal pronoun here (poetic word order). E connects the two coordinated abilities; puoi andar repeats the modal + infinitive pattern for “you can go/walk.” Tra elli means “among them,” with the archaic pronoun elli (them).
segments.purgatorio-27-g162: Non aspettar is a negative command addressed to thee; Dante often uses the infinitive form as an imperative (aspettar ~ aspettare) meaning “do not expect.” Mio dir is a nominalized infinitive (my saying / my word), and più here means “any longer” / “more.” Né mio cenno adds “nor (even) my signal/gesture,” cenno being a concrete noun “sign, nod.”
segments.purgatorio-27-g163: Libero is a predicate adjective standing alone as an exclamation/summary: “free.” It describes the state of the following noun (tuo arbitrio).
segments.purgatorio-27-g164: Dritto e sano are coordinate adjectives “upright and sound.” È is the 3rd-person singular copula linking those adjectives to tuo arbitrio (your discretion/will), so the phrase reads “your will is upright and sound.”
segments.purgatorio-27-g165: E fallo fora non fare a suo senno is a compact, slightly archaic clause: fallo is a noun “a fault/mistake,” fora is an archaic conditional/past-subjunctive form meaning “would be,” and non fare is the infinitive “not to act/do.” A suo senno means “according to its (i.e. the will’s) judgement.” Together: “and it would be a fault not to act according to its judgment.”
segments.purgatorio-27-g166: Per ch’io te sovra te corono e mitrio means “therefore I crown and mitre you over yourself.” Per ch’io here introduces the reason/result (“for which / therefore I”), sovra te is a prepositional phrase “over you/above you,” and the verb pair corono e mitrio (I crown and I mitre) takes te as direct object. Note: corono is 1st-person present “I crown” (from coronare) and mitrio is the noun/verb form for placing a mitre (ecclesiastical headgear) on someone; both verbs share the object te.

sentences.purgatorio-27-s18: Then he smiled, as one does to a child who is overcome by the apple.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s19: Then he set himself in front of me inside the fire, begging Statius to come behind, who earlier had parted us along a long road.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s20: As soon as I was inside, I would have thrown myself into a seething glass to cool off, so great was the fire there without measure.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s9: Believe me for certain that if you were to remain well inside the inmost part of this flame for a full thousand years,
sentences.purgatorio-27-s10: And if you perhaps think that I deceive you, go toward her, and make yourself be believed by touching with your own hands the hem of your garments.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s11: Put aside now, put aside every fear; turn here and come: enter confidently!
sentences.purgatorio-27-s5: I stretched out my hands upward, staring at the fire and vividly imagining human bodies I had already seen burning.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s6: The good escorts turned toward me; and Virgil said to me: "My son, here there may be torment, but not death."
sentences.purgatorio-27-s8: And if I guided you safely up on Geryon, what will I do now that is closer to God?
sentences.purgatorio-27-s21: My sweet father, to comfort me, kept on speaking of Beatrice, saying, "I seem already to see her eyes."
sentences.purgatorio-27-s22: A voice that was singing over there was guiding us; and we, still attentive to it, came out to where it rose.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s23: 'Come, blessed of my Father,' sounded within a light that was there, so that it overcame me and I could not look.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s24: "The sun is going," he added, "and evening comes; do not stop, but mind your step while the west has not yet darkened."
sentences.purgatorio-27-s1: Just as when the first rays tremble where their maker scattered the blood — the Ebro falling beneath high Libra, and the waves of the Ganges from the ninth are dried up — so the sun stood; whence the day was passing away, as the angel of God appeared to us joyful.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s2: Outside the flame he stood upon the bank, and he sang, 'Beati mundo corde!'
sentences.purgatorio-27-s3: in a voice far more alive than our own.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s4: Afterward: 'No more may you go on, unless first the fire bite, O holy souls; enter into it, and be not deaf to the singing over there,' he said to us as we were near him; whereupon I became such, when I heard it, as is one who is put into the pit.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s37: Such eagerness upon eagerness came over me from being up there, that at every step afterward I felt my feathers growing for flight.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s38: When the whole stairway below us had been run and we were upon the upper step, Virgil fixed his eyes on me and said: "You have seen the temporal fire and the eternal, my son; and you have come to the point where I, for my part, can discern no further."
sentences.purgatorio-27-s39: I have brought you here with intellect and with art; henceforth take your pleasure as your leader; you are off the steep paths, you are off art.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s40: Behold the sun that shines upon your brow; behold the little grasses, the flowers and the small shrubs that the earth here produces of its own accord.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s33: She takes pleasure in looking with her lovely eyes, just as I (take pleasure) in adorning myself with my hands; her seeing it, and my doing it, satisfies me.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s34: And already, by the pre-dawn gleams—which are so pleasing to pilgrims when they rise, inasmuch as, on returning, they lodge less far away—the darkness fled from every side, and my sleep with it; whereupon I rose, seeing the great masters already up.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s35: "That sweet fruit, which the care of mortals seeks along so many branches, will today put your hungers at rest."
sentences.purgatorio-27-s36: Virgil turned toward me and used such words; and never were there gifts that could be equal in pleasure to these.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s29: Only a little of the daylight outside was visible there; but, from that little, I could make out the stars they were accustomed to, and they appeared clearer and larger.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s30: So, musing and gazing at them, sleep took hold of me; sleep which often, before the deed is done, already knows the news.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s31: At that hour, I believe, when from the East first shone upon Mount Cytherea — which seems ever burning with the flame of love — a young and beautiful woman appeared to me in a dream, walking across a plain and gathering flowers; and singing she said: "Let whoever asks my name know that I am Leah, and I go moving my fair hands about to make myself a garland."
sentences.purgatorio-27-s32: To please myself at the looking-glass, here I adorn myself; but my sister Rachel never frees herself from her wonder, and sits all day.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s25: The road climbed straight up through the rock toward that side which removed from before me the rays of the sun, which was already low.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s26: And after a few steps we quickened our pace; because of the shadow that was cast we — both I and my companions — felt/perceived the sun setting behind us.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s27: And before the whole horizon, in all its immense parts, had assumed a single appearance, and night had taken up all its provisions, each of us made a bed on a step; for the nature of the mountain cut off from us the power to climb further and the pleasure.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s28: Like goats that stand chewing the cud in their holdings, swift and headstrong on the forward peaks before they have fed, silent in the shade while the sun boils, watched by the shepherd who is leaning on his staff and gives them a respite; and like the herdsman who shelters outside, along his flock he quietly spends the night, keeping watch so that no wild beast scatter it; such were all three of us then — I like a goat, and he like the shepherds, wrapped on this side and that by the high grotto.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s13: And I remained nevertheless firm, even though it was against my conscience.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s14: When he saw me standing there still, firm and stubborn, he—somewhat disturbed—said: "Now look, my son: between Beatrice and you there is this wall."
sentences.purgatorio-27-s15: As Pyramus opened his eyelid toward death at the name of Thisbe, and looked back at her when the mulberry turned crimson; so, my hardness made soft, I turned to the wise duke, hearing the name that ever sprouts again in my mind.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s16: Whereupon he shook his brow and said: "How!"
sentences.purgatorio-27-s41: While the joyful, beautiful eyes — which, weeping, caused me to come to you — are coming, you may sit and you may walk among them.
sentences.purgatorio-27-s42: Do not expect more of my words or of my signal; your will is free, upright and sound, and it would be a fault not to act according to its judgment: therefore I crown and mitre you over yourself.
